message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: The report is generated ONE-SHOT from the FULL RAW retrieved corpus, not from the distilled notes the agent used while searching. Tavily deliberately re-injects raw sources only at the writing step to avoid information loss.
DETAIL: This is the single load-bearing architectural claim in Tavily's engineering blog (Michael Griff, Dean Sacoransky, Noah Nefsky; Nov 24, 2025). Verbatim: 'deep research agents should be designed in a similar manner, in that tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and ONLY the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller. Similar to humans, it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss.' And: 'Only once we've gathered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources, using them as references to assemble the finished product.' So: search loop context = reflections only (cheap); writer context = raw web content (expensive, but once). This is what buys the token claim, and it means the writer sees a UNIFIED GLOBAL CONTEXT of all sources at once -- structurally the opposite of a per-section writer that only sees its own section's sources.
SYSTEM: Tavily
SOURCE GIVEN: https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this against the primary source.

input.prompt: Quote verbatim any passages about: (1) distilling tool outputs into reflections and only using reflections as context for the tool caller; (2) providing raw information as context at the point of preparing the final deliverable to avoid information loss; (3) returning to original sources when producing the final deliverable; (4) how the final report is written (one-shot from full corpus vs per-section writers); (5) DeepResearch Bench scores/RACE judge. Also give the article title, authors, and date.

input.query: Tavily deep research blog "reflections" raw sources final deliverable DeepResearch Bench

content.content:
# Article Summary

**Title:** Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art

**Authors:** Michael Griff, Dean Sacoransky & Noah Nefsky

**Date:** November 24, 2025

## Verbatim Passages

**(1) Distilling tool outputs into reflections:**
"tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and **only** the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller."

**(2) Providing raw information at final deliverable stage:**
"only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss."

**(3) Returning to original sources:**
"Once we've gathered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources, using them as references to assemble the finished product."

**(4) & (5) Final report writing approach and DeepResearch Bench scores:**
The article does not specify whether reports are written one-shot or per-section. Regarding DeepResearch Bench, it only states: "achieve[d] SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" without providing specific scores or mentioning a "RACE judge."

Web search results for query: "Tavily deep research blog "reflections" raw sources final deliverable DeepResearch Bench"

Links: [{"title":"Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art | Tavily Blog","url":"https://blog.tavily.com/research-en/"},{"title":"Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art","url":"https://huggingface.co/blog/Tavily/tavily-deep-research"},{"title":"Tavily 101: AI-powered Search for Developers | Tavily Blog","url":"https://www.tavily.com/blog/tavily-101-ai-powered-search-for-developers"},{"title":"Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.20843"},{"title":"Introducing DeepResearch: SoTA AI-Native Research API | Valyu","url":"https://www.valyu.ai/blogs/introducing-deepresearch"},{"title":"Open Deep Research","url":"https://www.together.ai/blog/open-deep-research"},{"title":"How NVIDIA AI-Q Reached \\#1 on DeepResearch Bench I and II","url":"https://huggingface.co/blog/nvidia/how-nvidia-won-deepresearch-bench"},{"title":"ViDR: Grounding Multimodal Deep Research Reports in Source Visual Evidence","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.13034"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"Deep Research AI Workflow Using Langgraph + Tavily + Any LLM Provider | by Gaurav Sharma | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@gaurav219688/deep-research-ai-workflow-using-langgraph-tavily-search-any-llm-provider-373ae5aa2cfd"}]

I found relevant information from your search query. Here are the key findings:

## Tavily's Deep Research Blog Post - Key Concepts

Tavily's deep research design philosophy distills tool outputs into reflections, and only uses past reflections as context for the tool caller until preparing the final deliverable, at which point raw information is provided as context to ensure no information loss.

Through this methodology, Tavily reduced token consumption by 66% (compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench.

## The Reflections and Raw Sources Approach

The approach mirrors how humans research in an iterative, unstructured way: defining the task, gathering data and extracting key insights into short-term memory, repeating this cycle, and only returning to original sources as references when assembling the final product.

The Tavily blog post (published November 24-25, 2025) titled "Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art" details this architecture where reflections serve as the primary context mechanism throughout the research process, with raw sources being reintroduced only at the final deliverable stage.

Does the report get written in ONE shot from the full raw corpus of all sources at once, or is it written section-by-section (per-section writers / parallel sub-agents each seeing only their own sources)? input.query: DeepResearch Bench leaderboard 2026 RACE judge GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro

content.content:
# Agent Architecture & Report Generation Analysis

Based on the provided text, here's what's explicitly stated about the report generation process:

## Report Writing Approach

The article describes a single-phase writing approach:

**"only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context"**

This indicates the report is generated **in one shot** from accumulated source materials, not section-by-section with parallel sub-agents.

## Context Management During Research

The key architectural principle involves distilling information iteratively:

**"tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and only the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller"**

The research phase uses compressed reflections to guide further investigation, while the generation phase receives full raw sources.

## Performance Metrics Mentioned

- **Token reduction**: "reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research)"
- **Benchmark position**: Achieved "SOTA on DeepResearch Bench"

## Notably Absent

The document does **not** explicitly describe:
- Sub-agents or parallel writers
- Section-by-section generation
- Outline creation as a separate step
- Context isolation strategies between parallel processes

The architecture appears deliberately simplified—leveraging single-pass generation from distilled research insights rather than distributed or hierarchical writing approaches.

Web search results for query: "DeepResearch Bench leaderboard 2026 RACE judge GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization for Open-Ended Deep Research","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.20122"},{"title":"VeriTrace: Evolving Mental Models for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.26081"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/"},{"title":"Benchmarking LLM-as-a-Judge for Long-Form Output Evaluation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.01629"},{"title":"Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.18267"},{"title":"Chaining the Evidence: Robust Reinforcement Learning for Deep Search Agents with Citation-Aware Rubric Rewards","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.06021"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench - a Hugging Face Space by Ayanami0730","url":"https://huggingface.co/spaces/Ayanami0730/DeepResearch-Leaderboard"},{"title":"AgentDisCo: Towards Disentanglement and Collaboration in Open-ended Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.11732"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the DeepResearch Bench leaderboard and the recent changes to its RACE judge:

Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepResearch Bench benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = 68.78%).

All three candidates exceeded this baseline by 1.3–3 points, with GPT-5.5 winning on Overall, PAR, and FAS. GPT-5.5 was adopted as the new RACE evaluator (with GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline).

The benchmark accepts submissions evaluated under both the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and the new one (GPT-5.5), with results displayed on two separate leaderboards so the rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator.

The GPT-5.5 leaderboard status was still under construction as of the announcement, with an expected launch within a week alongside the migrated scores.

content.text: The WebFetch summarizer is *inferring* one-shot rather than quoting it. I need the raw text to avoid trusting a paraphrase.

input.command: cd /tmp && curl -sL "https://blog.tavily.com/research-en/" -o t1.html; curl -sL "https://huggingface.co/blog/Tavily/tavily-deep-research" -o t2.html; wc -c t1.html t2.html
input.description: Fetch raw HTML of both copies of the Tavily blog post

input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 -c "
import re,html
for f in ['t1.html','t2.html']:
    s=open(f,encoding='utf-8',errors='ignore').read()
    s=re.sub(r'(?is)<(script|style|nav|svg)[^>]*>.*?</\1>',' ',s)
    s=re.sub(r'(?s)<[^>]+>','\n',s)
    s=html.unescape(s)
    lines=[l.strip() for l in s.split('\n') if len(l.strip())>2]
    open(f+'.txt','w').write('\n'.join(lines))
    print(f, len(lines))
"

26:Research agents are rapidly becoming one of the most important applications of AI. Research is a foundational knowledge-work task: collecting, reading, and synthesizing information underpins everything from writing and decision-making to coding itself. Yet human-driven research is constrained by memory, reading speed, and time. AI research agents, by contrast, can process vast amounts of information, synthesize insights instantly, and scale effortlessly. Because of this, research agents are emerging as a top use case for AI today and will soon become a core subcomponent of broader agentic workflows across content generation, coding, sales, and more. In this post, we share the technical and philosophical lessons we’ve learned building a 37:Over the last seven months, model capabilities have quietly but meaningfully evolved (especially in their tool-calling abilities). This single optimization focus has pushed us from workflows to agents. We believe future models will be trained to solve the current pain points of agent developers. Every model is ultimately consumed by a harness, so models should evolve in service of that harness. We hope to see models improve in high-recall summarization (for context compression), tool-calling reliability, and concision in writing. 46:Focus on context engineering (more on this in the next section). 48:Long-horizon research tasks expose a fundamental challenge in current agent design: the task of maintaining a clean, optimized context window over time. If curating context is not a task the engineer pays close attention to, the agent is almost destined for failure. The following outlines our thinking around this concept within the deep research domain. 50:Using Tavily’s Advanced Search is the natural first step to take in overcoming this challenge, in that it abstracts away the processing of raw web content and returns only the most relevant content chunks from each source. In leveraging this functionality, we let Tavily Search do the heavy lifting and allow Tavily Research to reap the benefit, gathering the most valuable content in a latency-efficient manner. 51:Ensuring that the agent does not overfit to a single research thread is the next step towards an effective context-gathering pipeline. It is in this regard that global state persistence and source deduplication is paramount, and in our case, it helps threefold: 54:It lends to effective source attribution later on in the generation process. 57:Humans research in an inherently unstructured, iterative way. We start by defining the task: what we’re trying to accomplish and what information we need. We next gather data from our sources, extracting the key insights and holding them in short-term memory, letting these distilled thoughts guide our subsequent actions. 58:This cycle repeats: collect information, distill it, decide what to do next. Only once we’ve gathered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources, using them as references to assemble the finished product. 59:We believe that deep research agents should be designed in a similar manner, in that tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and 61:the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller. Similar to humans, it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss. 78:– the intersection of quality and efficiency in full effect. 95:What is r in your solution ? Is it a scratch pad that is updated in the LLM context between each round of research ?

content.text: The verbatim quotes are confirmed word-for-word. Now the load-bearing part — let me read the full body.

content.content:
Follow Tavily Noah Nefsky noahnefsky Follow Tavily Research agents are rapidly becoming one of the most important applications of AI. Research is a foundational knowledge-work task: collecting, reading, and synthesizing information underpins everything from writing and decision-making to coding itself. Yet human-driven research is constrained by memory, reading speed, and time. AI research agents, by contrast, can process vast amounts of information, synthesize insights instantly, and scale effortlessly. Because of this, research agents are emerging as a top use case for AI today and will soon become a core subcomponent of broader agentic work state-of-the-art research agent , and where we believe the field is headed. Building for the Future Agent Harness The task of building an agent harness is to create a software layer that enhances a model’s runtime execution through context management, tool invocations, loop control, orchestration, and error handling. Building applications on top of rapidly improving models is, however, a modern engineering challenge. How can we design software today that absorbs the performance gains from future model releases? This requires forecasting how models will evolve, staying optimistic about their progress, limiting assumptions, and avoiding hand-crafted optimizations. We learned this the hard way seven months ago, when we had to abandon our first attempt at deep research and rebuild the entire system from scratch. The first architecture was complicated and sophisticated (we thought this was a good thing), but its assumptions became bottlenecks when the next generation of models arrived. Models Over the last seven months, model capabilities have quietly but meaningfully evolved (especially in their tool-calling abilities). This single optimization focus has pushed us from workflows to agents. We believe future models will be trained to solve the current pain points of agent developers. Every model is ultimately consumed by a harness, so models should evolve in service of that harness. We hope to see models improve in high-recall summarization (for context compression), tool-calling reliability, and concision in writing. Tools Similarly, tools should evolve to support LLMs and widely adopted agent harnesses. The best tools should perform some context engineering on the tool side, abstracted away from the agent. They should return only the most relevant data instead of dumping large volumes of tokens into the context window. As a tool provider, we’ve invested heavily in our advanced search feature, which has context engineering baked in. This in turn lowers hallucinations and latency for the downstream agent processes. Takeaways To build agents that improve over time, we followed a few guiding principles: Simplify orchestration logic and lean into autonomy. Pay close attention to what models and tools are being optimized for, and leverage their emerging capabilities. Focus on context engineering (more on this in the next section). Context Engineering — An Exercise in Curation Long-horizon research tasks expose a fundamental challenge in current agent design: the task of maintaining a clean, optimized context window over time. If curating context is not a task the engineer pays close attention to, the agent is almost destined for failure. The following outlines our thinking around this concept within the deep research domain. Context-Managed Web Retrieval Using Tavily’s Advanced Search is the natural first step to take in overcoming this challenge, in that it abstracts away the processing of raw web content and returns only the most relevant content chunks from each source. In leveraging this functionality, we let Tavily Search do the heavy lifting and allow Tavily Research to reap the benefit, gathering the most valuable content in a latency-efficient manner. Ensuring that the agent does not overfit to a single research thread is the next step towards an effective context-gathering pipeline. It is in this regard that global state persistence and source deduplication is paramount, and in our case, it helps threefold: It ensures the agent is exposed only to fresh information. It allows the engineer to recognize when the information scope is narrowing and to prompt the agent to explore untapped relevant domains. It lends to effective source attribution later on in the generation process. At Tavily, interacting with the web is our bread and butter. Architecting a refined web-retrieval system engineered for deep research was a foundational building block for our deep research agent design as a whole. Modeling the Human-Web Interaction Humans research in an inherently unstructured, iterative way. We start by defining the task: what we’re trying to accomplish and what information we need. We next gather data from our sources, extracting the key insights and holding them in short-term memory, letting these distilled thoughts guide our subsequent actions. This cycle repeats: collect information, distill it, decide what to do next. Only once we’ve gathered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources, using them as references to assemble the finished product. We believe that deep research agents should be designed in a similar manner, in that tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and only the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller. Similar to humans, it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss. Doing More with Less This approach differs from traditional context structuring in a ReAct agent-based architecture. Typically, tool calls and outputs are propagated through the tool calling loop, with previously retrieved/generated tokens being persisted in the context window on each subsequent iteration. This pattern can be seen in LangChain’s Open Deep Research agent implementation, and from a token consumption perspective, it can be modeled by the following quadratic series, where is the amount of tokens the tool calling model is invoked with on each tool calling iteration, and is the number of tool calling iterations. n + 2n + 3n + \cdots + mn \;=\; n \cdot \frac{m(m+1)}{2} Contrarily, our proposed method of context engineering removes this token propagation (as the knowledge distillations, even when aggregated, are negligible when compared to the quantity of tokens gathered from web) and can be modeled by the following linear series. n + n + n + \cdots + n \;=\; nm When comparing the two approaches, tokens are saved on a per-agent basis by a factor of \frac{m+1}{2} , and when extrapolating this over a multi-agent system and with consumption at scale, the absolute value of tokens saved becomes even more significant. Through this methodology, we were able to reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench – the intersection of quality and efficiency in full effect. Productionizing Agents — an Ongoing Challenge Building production-grade agents is a balancing act. We leaned into autonomy to maximize performance and quality, while still meeting strict requirements for latency, cost, and reliability. Engineering with Non-Determinism LLMs are inherently non-deterministic, and we found that giving them guard-railed freedom to reason and iterate produces the strongest results. Autonomy, when gone wrong, can cause agent behavior to go off track. Tools can be called incorrectly, LLMs can overfit to a subtopic, and expected reasoning patterns may break. No single safeguard will catch all of these issues. A shift in engineering mindset is required: treat failure modes as core design considerations, not afterthoughts. Simple guardrails like tool-call retries or model cascades help, but proactively anticipating anomalies, reinforcing proper patterns in prompting and edge-case testing is what enables production-grade, long-running agents. Optimal Tooling — Less is More From our experience, it’s better to expose a small, essential toolset to the agent rather than a large, complex one. We were tempted to over-engineer by adding many tools that seemed useful in theory, but in practice this created new failure modes and made it harder for LLMs to consistently choose the right tool and iterate effectively. Evals We used evals to steer our development process but also recognize their shortcomings. LLM-as-a-judge evals are difficult to trust: current models are non‑deterministic, uninterpretable in their reasoning, and can turn into bottlenecks, especially for long‑running agents where a single experiment can take days to complete. Rather than optimizing for benchmark scores, we optimized for directional feedback. The core question was always: did this change make the agent more reliable and more useful in practice? Evals became a tool for validating that direction, not the optimization target. Intuition and careful agent‑trace monitoring consistently provided higher‑signal feedback than any single eval score. Overall, the best outcome is rarely the highest numerical score. For production systems, improvements like reduced token usage, reliability, lower latency, and fewer failures are more valuable than a one‑point bump on an eval. If you’re interested in experiencing the result of these findings in practice, you can sign up for early access to Tavily Research here Spaces mentioned in this article Community What is r in your solution ? Is it a scratch pad that is updated in the LLM context between each round of research ?

Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art
Research agents are becoming a cornerstone of modern knowledge work. In this post, we share the technical and philosophical lessons behind building a state-of-the-art deep research agent—covering agent harness design, context engineering, and how we achieved dramatically lower token usage while reaching SOTA performance.
Michael Griff, Dean Sacoransky & Noah Nefsky
November 24, 2025
Research agents are rapidly becoming one of the most important applications of AI. Research is a foundational knowledge-work task: collecting, reading, and synt

content.text:
I have the full primary text of both copies. The verdict is clear.

**What checks out:** Both quotes are verbatim-accurate, word for word. Article metadata confirmed: "Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art," Michael Griff, Dean Sacoransky & Noah Nefsky, November 24, 2025, at both `www.tavily.com/blog/research-en` (HTTP 200) and the HF mirror.

**What does not:** The claim's DETAIL — the load-bearing part — is an extrapolation the article never makes. Word-frequency probe over the complete text:

| term | occurrences |
|---|---|
| "one-shot" / "one shot" / "single pass" | **0** |
| "unified" / "global context" / "corpus" | **0** |
| "writer" / "sub-agent" / "parallel" | **0** |

The article contains **no description of the report-generation step whatsoever**. It is a ~4-minute philosophical post about the *search loop*. So "structurally the opposite of a per-section writer" isn't refuted by the source — the source is simply silent, and cannot adjudicate it.

Three independent problems beyond mere silence:

1. **"FULL RAW corpus" is contradicted by the same article.** Tavily's retrieval layer explicitly pre-filters: Advanced Search *"abstracts away the processing of raw web content and returns only the most relevant content chunks from each source."* So what gets re-injected at write time is already-distilled chunks, not a raw corpus. The claim's "writer context = raw web content (expensive)" is wrong on the "raw" dimension.

2. **The quote is normative, not descriptive.** "deep research agents **should** be designed…", "**your** agent", "**your** tool caller." It's advice to the reader. And the second quote ("Only once we've gathered enough understanding…") sits inside the *human analogy* paragraph ("Humans research in an inherently unstructured, iterative way. We start by…") — it describes people, not Tavily's system.

3. **"The single load-bearing architectural claim" is misidentified.** What buys the 66% token reduction is the quadratic-vs-linear math on the **tool-calling loop** (`n·m(m+1)/2` → `n·m`), i.e. *not propagating tool outputs*. The write step is a **cost** in that model, not a saving. The article also explicitly invokes *"a multi-agent system"* when extrapolating — mildly cutting against a single unified writer.

**On currency:** the post asserts only "achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" with zero numbers. That standing predates the 2026 evaluator migration (Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecated June 17, 2026; GPT-5.5 adopted as RACE judge, GPT-5.4-mini for FACT), so any Nov-2025 SOTA claim is legacy-evaluator-only. The claim under test is architectural, so this doesn't falsify it — but it means the post cannot be cited for a current leaderboard position either.

input.reasoning:
The two verbatim quotes are CONFIRMED — I pulled the full raw text from both https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en (HTTP 200) and the HuggingFace mirror (huggingface.co/blog/Tavily/tavily-deep-research), and both quoted passages appear word for word. Article metadata is also confirmed: "Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art," Michael Griff, Dean Sacoransky & Noah Nefsky, November 24, 2025.

But the CLAIM and its DETAIL are an extrapolation that the source does not make, and the load-bearing inference fails on four counts:

1. THE ARTICLE NEVER DESCRIBES THE WRITER AT ALL. Word probe over the complete text: "one-shot" = 0, "one shot" = 0, "single pass" = 0, "unified" = 0, "global context" = 0, "corpus" = 0, "writer" = 0, "sub-agent"/"subagent" = 0, "parallel" = 0. ("section" x2 = "the next section"; "outline" x1 = the verb "outlines our thinking".) The post is a ~4-minute philosophical piece about the SEARCH LOOP and context engineering. It contains no description of the report-generation step whatsoever. Therefore the assertion that the writer "sees a UNIFIED GLOBAL CONTEXT of all sources at once -- structurally the opposite of a per-section writer" cannot be sourced here; the article is silent and cannot adjudicate between one-shot and per-section.

2. "FULL RAW retrieved corpus" is CONTRADICTED by the same article. Tavily's retrieval layer explicitly pre-filters before anything reaches the agent: "Using Tavily's Advanced Search ... abstracts away the processing of raw web content and returns only the most relevant content chunks from each source." So whatever is re-injected at write time is already-distilled relevant chunks, not the full raw corpus. The claim's gloss "writer context = raw web content (expensive)" is wrong on the "raw" dimension.

3. THE QUOTE IS NORMATIVE, NOT DESCRIPTIVE. The passage is advice to the reader, not a spec of Tavily's shipped system: "deep research agents SHOULD be designed in a similar manner", "YOUR tool caller", "YOUR agent". And the second quote ("Only once we've gathered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources") sits inside the human-analogy paragraph ("Humans research in an inherently unstructured, iterative way. We start by defining the task..."), describing how PEOPLE research — not Tavily's architecture.

4. THE "SINGLE LOAD-BEARING ARCHITECTURAL CLAIM" IS MISIDENTIFIED. What actually buys the token claim is the quadratic-vs-linear math on the tool-calling loop (n + 2n + ... + mn = n*m(m+1)/2, reduced to n*m), i.e. NOT propagating tool outputs through the ReAct loop; savings factor (m+1)/2, yielding "reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research)". The raw-reinjection-at-write-time step is a COST in that model, not a saving. The article also explicitly says "when extrapolating this over a multi-agent system", which mildly cuts against a single unified global writer.

CURRENCY: The post makes no numeric DeepResearch Bench claim at all — only "achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench," with no score, no RACE breakdown, no dated leaderboard position. That standing predates the 2026 evaluator migration (Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecated June 17, 2026; GPT-5.5 adopted as the new RACE evaluator, GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline, with legacy and new results kept on separate leaderboards). The claim under test is architectural rather than a score claim, so the migration does not falsify it — but the post cannot be cited for any current leaderboard position, and the blog itself disclaims benchmark-chasing ("Rather than optimizing for benchmark scores, we optimized for directional feedback... the best outcome is rarely the highest numerical score").

Net: the quotes are real; the architecture conclusion drawn from them is not in the source and is partly contradicted by it. Per the "default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm from a primary source" instruction, this is UNSUPPORTED — the primary source confirms the quotations but not the claim.
input.corrected:
What the primary source (https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en, Griff/Sacoransky/Nefsky, Nov 24 2025) actually supports, and only this:

(a) VERBATIM AND ACCURATE: "We believe that deep research agents should be designed in a similar manner, in that tool outputs should be distilled into reflections, and only the set of past reflections should be used as context for your tool caller. Similar to humans, it is only at the point when your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable that you must provide the raw information as context, so as to ensure there is no information loss." This is PRESCRIPTIVE ("should", "your agent") — a design principle offered to readers, not a description of Tavily's shipped writer.

(b) The second quote is slightly misattributed in the claim. Full sentence, from the paragraph about how HUMANS research: "This cycle repeats: collect information, distill it, decide what to do next. Only once we've gathered enough understanding to produce the final deliverable do we return to the original sources, using them as references to assemble the finished product."

(c) The "raw information" re-injected at write time is NOT a raw corpus. The article states Tavily's Advanced Search "abstracts away the processing of raw web content and returns only the most relevant content chunks from each source." Retrieval is pre-filtered before the agent ever sees it.

(d) The real load-bearing claim is the loop math, not the writer: token consumption modeled as quadratic (n + 2n + ... + mn = n*m(m+1)/2) for a standard ReAct agent such as LangChain's Open Deep Research, versus linear (n*m) for Tavily's reflection-only loop — a saving factor of (m+1)/2, reported as "reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench."

(e) The article contains ZERO mentions of one-shot generation, single-pass writing, a unified/global writer context, a "corpus", per-section writers, sub-agents, outlines, or parallelism in the writing step. It does once reference "a multi-agent system" when extrapolating the token savings. Any statement about whether Tavily's writer is one-shot-global versus per-section is NOT sourceable from this blog and must be dropped or sourced elsewhere.

(f) STALENESS: the post gives no DeepResearch Bench numbers, only an undated "SOTA" assertion under the legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro RACE evaluator. DeepResearch Bench replaced that judge with GPT-5.5 (FACT pipeline on GPT-5.4-mini) following Google's June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, and now maintains separate legacy/new leaderboards (github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench; huggingface.co/spaces/Ayanami0730/DeepResearch-Leaderboard). Do not cite this blog for a current leaderboard standing.
